text: The surgeon makes a vertical incision at the vaginal introitus, carefully dissecting the rectovaginal septum. The septum is circumferentially freed from the fistula orifice to facilitate repair.
The surgeon repairs the rectovaginal fistula from the vaginal side using interrupted 2-0 absorbable sutures, following a curettage of the fistula orifice from the rectal side.

The surgeon makes a vertical left paravaginal incision, seven centimeters in length, to access and carefully mobilize the bulbocavernosus muscle along with its surrounding fibroadipose tissue. The posterolateral blood supply from the internal pudendal artery is preserved during this process.

The surgeon transposes the isolated bulbocavernosus muscle into a prepared subcutaneous tunnel, ensuring well-vascularized tissue to protect the direct repair. The distal limb of the flap is cut to prevent bulking at the rectovaginal septum, which could cause dyspareunia. The graft is fixed with interrupted two-zero absorbable sutures, and the vertical paravaginal incision is closed, leaving a pain rose drain in place.
